The Pole is the climax toward which the entire novel builds. After crossing the ice and reaching open water, the explorers sight the fiery peak, and at last "the North Pole was indeed before his eyes" (ACH-055-024). Hatteras, half-mad with triumph, plants his flag and cries out that the ground, air, and sea about them all belong to the Pole.

Amsterdam Island is the real Indian Ocean island lying on the 37th parallel that Glenarvan's searchers consider as they trace Captain Grant's course. It is "the only group of islands found on this route, the Amsterdam Isles." (SC-027-064)
Later travelers reckon their bearings by it, though the ground has grown treacherous: they "lost their way, and turned to the south when they thought they were near to Walruses' Bay." (FC-022-048)

- Falls of Niagara

Every passenger is sorted by whether Pekin is their end. Ephrinell and the Englishwoman are marked as "going to Pekin," (ASC-004-005) as is "that Russian major who is sitting near the captain of the Astara." (ASC-004-029) The reporter counts the whole span of his ride "from Tiflis to Pekin." (ASC-004-058)
